name: Honda Pilot
reliability: 7/10 — the third-gen Pilot is a comfortable, spacious three-row family SUV riding on Honda's durable J35 V6, and the mechanicals generally go the distance. The knocks are familiar Honda-V6 ones: Variable Cylinder Management (VCM) is tied to oil consumption, spark-plug fouling, and low-speed drivetrain vibration from worn mounts, and the early ZF 9-speed automatic (Touring/Elite) drew complaints for rough, hesitant shifts before software updates. Buy one with the recalls closed and the VCM understood and it's a dependable hauler.
cost_to_own: Insurance: Moderate (large family SUV) · Maintenance ~$550/yr · Fuel economy is average for a three-row · Strong resale — three-row Hondas hold value well
popular_mods: A VCM disabler (VCMTuner / S-VCM) — the go-to J35 mod to keep all six cylinders firing, curb oil use, and spare the engine mounts
All-weather floor liners + a cargo liner for the third-row/trunk area — essential for family and dog duty
Roof crossbars + a cargo box, plus a trailer hitch (AWD tows up to ~5,000 lbs)
A second set of wheels with dedicated winter tires — the best foul-weather upgrade for the i-VTM4 AWD
Honest take: the Pilot is a family SUV — money is better spent on the VCM disabler, good tires, and maintenance than on power
oil_interval: Per the Maintenance Minder (~7,500 miles) — check the level between changes on VCM engines

generations.2nd Gen (2009–2015 US): The boxy, truck-like Pilot — 3.5L J35 V6 (~250 hp), 5-speed automatic, and a reputation for going forever. Rugged and roomy; the value pick if you want simple and durable.
generations.3rd Gen (2016–2022 US): A car-based, more refined redesign — 280 hp J35, 6-speed automatic (LX/EX/EX-L) or a ZF 9-speed (Touring/Elite), i-VTM4 torque-vectoring AWD, and seating for up to eight. The mainstream family-SUV sweet spot.
generations.4th Gen (2023+ US): Bigger and more rugged-looking with a 285 hp J35, a 10-speed automatic, and an off-road-flavored TrailSport trim. More capable, but pricier — a clean third-gen is the used-market value.

common_issues: VCM-related oil consumption, spark-plug fouling, and low-speed vibration from worn engine/transmission mounts on the J35 — the defining thing to know about this drivetrain
Early ZF 9-speed automatic (2016–2018 Touring/Elite) hesitation and harsh shifts, plus an unpopular push-button shifter — most improved with software updates
Idle stop / auto start-stop can feel shuddery on restart — normal, but a common gripe
Infotainment on 2016–2018 was laggy and lacked a volume knob (the knob returned in the 2019 refresh)
Automatic emergency braking (CMBS) false activations reported by some owners
maintenance_tips: Change the 0W-20 oil on time and check the level between changes — VCM engines can use oil quietly
A VCM disabler is the most-recommended step for engine-mount life and oil-consumption peace of mind
Service the transmission fluid with the correct Honda ATF on schedule — the 9-speed especially dislikes neglect
Refresh spark plugs on schedule (VCM can foul them) and inspect the engine mounts if you feel low-speed shudder
Confirm all open recalls are closed by VIN, and budget for brakes given the vehicle's weight
